docs.title: Welcome
docs.text:
Lia Ryan's Datasheet as part of Sable for Team 303

Submission: 05, 04, 2026

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: • The purpose of this website is to detail my contributions to Team 303's Sable drone.

docs.title: Project Summary
docs.text:
• Team 303's goal for Sable drone is to create a WIFI controlled ground exploration/rescue bot with an arm and grabber attached for sample collection. Sable should also be able to find injured or trapped people in a building and communicate with them.
  • To see more information on what our goal is with Sable, please visit my team report.

docs.title: My Contribution
docs.text:
• My subsystem is the Human Design Interface (HDI). The HDI will be able to control parameters from every other subsytem through a keypad and OLED screen. The OLED screen will also be used to view data being output from the other subsystems. Some examples of data that can be viewed are: Microphone input, Distance from the distance sensor, Camera status, and Wireless status.
  • Content should also help an unfamiliar reader navigate to areas of interest. Information like:

To review a list of my module's requirements, you can review it in the "Requirements" section of the datasheet.

To review information of my block diagram, you can review it in the "Block Diagram" section of the datasheet.

To review a list of major components in my subsystem, you can review it in the "Component Selection" section of the datasheet.

To review the microcontroller I'll be using and why I chose it, you can review it in the "Microcontroller Selection" section of the datasheet.

To review the details listed of the material used to construct the subsection, you can review it in the "BOM" section of the datasheet.

To review the details of the Human Design Interface schematic, you can review it in the "Schematic" section of the datasheet.

docs.title: Human Machine Interface Requirements
docs.text:
The following sections document the requirements that this Human Machine Interface module needs to fulfill to meet device requirements set by team 303 for our ground search and rescue drone and the mandated Human Machine Interface requirements for this class.

The Human Machine Interface is a required user interface on the bot that interacts with the whole subsystem using pushbuttons and an OLED screen.

Requirements Description Measure of Threshold Target Measure Stretch Requirement (Y-N) Surface mounted, 3.3V switching power regulator 3.2V 3.3V N Surface mounted microcontroller 1 PIC or ESP 8-bit PIC N Wireless Communication Bidirectional communication between vehicle and control Bidirectional communication between vehicle and control or status feed without wires Y Display live video Sends out 30 FPS per second Live video with less than 3 seconds of delay Y Human Design Interface; Respond manually with buttons Small OLED screen with pushbuttons that permits a user to view all sensor data in text; directly control the actuator; modify setpoints on all the system boards; toggle GPIO debug pins on ALL subsystems. All the content in MoT and ability to tune sensors and actuators with controls on exterior of device. N Daisy chain UART Daisy chain UART connection between all subsystems Separate UART loops for high speed communication through certain subsystems N Device must be able to communicate to survivors using a speaker Is able to playback audio sent from an HMI device and listen to its environment. Is able to playback audio sent from an HMI device and listen to its environment in a hearable range of 90 db, 2 - 5 kHz. Y Locomotion Control Directly controls locomotion for moving forward, reverse, and turning. Directly controls each wheel individually N Wireless Control Displays status and controls connection status for the wireless system. Displays bit rate and packets of wireless data transmission. Manual control over which wifi channel to use. N Arm Control User controls for general arm movement. Precise user controls for exact degree movement for arm motors. N Camera/distance sensor Control Displays camera and distance sensor status and readings. Ability to turn on and off camera and distance sensors. Ability to fine tune camera and distance sensor. N Microphone/Speaker Control Displays graphical output from microphone and input to speaker. Ability to turn microphone and speaker on and off. Ability to change microphone sensitivity and speaker volume. N

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This is the block diagram for the Human Design Interface subsystem for Exploration drone project.

The Human Design Interface (HDI) requires several parts in order to function within our device's requirements and the requirements of our class. The block diagram parts to fulfill these requirements follow.

  • UART upstream and downstream header      These headers are to communicate with the rest of the device's subsystems in a loop
  • OLED Screen      To display data, control, and alter the other subsystems
  • Snap programmer      Allows PIC to be reprogrammed
  • Keypad      User input for controlling device and reading outputs
  • Power Supply      Powers the subsystem(s)
  • LEDs      For debugging and any other purpose that we may want them to do

docs.title: Decision Making Process
docs.text: In order to develop this Block Diagram, I used information from the datasheets of the ESP32, Keypad, LED, and OLED display. This information showed me that connecting these components to these pins will allow my subsystem to function.

docs.title: Block Diagram
docs.text: The Human Design Interface Block Diagram that I made.

docs.title: Human Design Interface Selected Major Components
Human Design Interface Selected Major Components
docs.text: The following sections are the selected major components necessary for the Human Design Interface.

docs.title: OLED Screen
docs.text:
Solution Pros Cons Option 1. Winstar Display WEA012864DWPP3N000030.96” white graphic display$7.35/eachlink to product * Experience using in class lab* Less pins to worry about* Less expensive * Smaller display size* Difficult to view Option 2. Winstar Display WEA012864ALAP3N00E00 1.54” yellow graphic display$14.78/eachlink to product * Similar interface as OLED used in class* Larger Screen* Easier to view* Yellow color could look more unique * More pins to worry about* More expensive

Choice: Option 2: Winstar Display WEA012864ALAP3N00E00 1.54” yellow graphic display

Rationale: The main reason I chose this display is because it is larger than the one supplied to us in class. This will make it easier for us to see that data and words that are to be displayed on it. Also, this display uses the same I2C interface as the smaller one meaning that the programming of it should be on the same difficulty as the one supplied in class. However, there are more pins on this display, but they seem to be optional pins that allow for extra customization. The yellow color of the OLED could also help set us apart from our competitors.

docs.title: Keypad
docs.text:
Solution Pros Cons Option 1. Knitter SwitchYFT KNI 12B 19Membrane Keypad$14.44/eachlink to product * Slim and light* Easy to stick anywhere * Button feel may be lacking* More expensive* Harder to connect to the header Option 2. SparkFun Electronics14662Switch Keypad$4.95/eachlink to product * Less expensive* Holes to screw onto desired position* Easier to connect a pin header * May be bulky for our device* Less detailed datasheet

Choice: Option 2

Rationale: I chose option 2 due to the much lower price when compared to option 1; having tactile feedback in comparison to option 1; and having an easier connector to hook up to a header to go to the PCB board as compared to option 1.

docs.title: LEDs
docs.text:
Solution Pros Cons Option 1. KingbrightAM23ESGCGreen/Red LED$0.48/eachlink to product * Pin layout is easier to solder* Cheaper in bulk* Wider viewing angle* Uses less power* Smaller size N/A Option 2. SunLEDXZMDKVG57W-1Green/Red LED$0.48/eachlink to product N/A * Larger size* Pin layout is more difficult to solder* More expensive in bulk* Smaller viewing angle* Uses more power

Choice: Option 1

Rationale: I chose option 1 because the layout of the LED looks like it would be much easier to solder onto a PCB than that of option 2. Also, the viewing angle is much greater, which would make it easier to see the light when debugging.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This schematic, shown in Figure 1, is design to support human interfacing with Sable. Using a keypad and OLED screen. This subsystem allows the user to read data coming in from all the other subsystems while also being able to control any adjustable parameters that the other subsystems may have.

The user input will be taken in through a 12-key keypad. With so many buttons, this keypad will able to adapt to whatever type of control or adjustment is required of each subsystem. The OLED screen will be used to display data being sent from each subsystem and any variables that can be adjusted. Subsystem information will be cycled on the OLED screen using a button on the keypad.

Figure 1: Showing my Human Design Interface subsystem for Sable.

docs.title: API
docs.text: This section details the application programming interface (API) for the Human Design Interface subsytem for Sable that I am working on. The following will detail how all the messages being received by or sent out from my subsystem will be dealt with and how they will be structured. This includes the number of bytes, data type, variable name, the smallest and largest number my subsystem will recognize for each message, and an example of valid data.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
1. Start and finish PCB design earlier than you need do

  2. Keeping updated files on all of the computers you use is very important

  3. Soldering surface mount components is far easier with a heat gun.

  4. Communication protocols and data types being sent need to defined asap

  5. Test in increments to avoid costly mistakes

  6. Read the whole datasheet before confirming components

  7. Try to standardize components between subsystems

  8. Completing steps early leaves time for refinement

docs.title: Style 1
docs.text:
This is the example found in the assignment, uses more html

Table 1: Example component selection

External Clock Module

Solution Pros Cons Option 1. XC1259TR-ND surface mount crystal$1/eachlink to product * Inexpensive[^1]* Compatible with PSoC* Meets surface mount constraint of project * Requires external components and support circuitry for interface* Needs special PCB layout. * Option 2. * CTX936TR-ND surface mount oscillator * $1/each * Link to product * Outputs a square wave * Stable over operating temperature * Direct interface with PSoC (no external circuitry required) range * More expensive * Slow shipping speed

Choice: Option 2: CTX936TR-ND surface mount oscillator

Rationale: A clock oscillator is easier to work with because it requires no external circuitry in order to interface with the PSoC. This is particularly important because we are not sure of the electrical characteristics of the PCB, which could affect the oscillation of a crystal. While the shipping speed is slow, according to the website if we order this week it will arrive within 3 weeks.

docs.title: Style 2
docs.text:
Also acceptable, more markdown friendly

External Clock Module

  1. XC1259TR-ND surface mount crystal

    • $1/each
    • link to product
    Pros Cons Inexpensive Requires external components and support circuitry for interface Compatible with PSoC Needs special PCB layout. Meets surface mount constraint of project

  2. CTX936TR-ND surface mount oscillator

    • $1/each
    • Link to product
    Pros Cons Outputs a square wave More expensive Stable over operating temperature Slow shipping speed Direct interface with PSoC (no external circuitry required) range

Choice: Option 2: CTX936TR-ND surface mount oscillator

Rationale: A clock oscillator is easier to work with because it requires no external circuitry in order to interface with the PSoC. This is particularly important because we are not sure of the electrical characteristics of the PCB, which could affect the oscillation of a crystal. While the shipping speed is slow, according to the website if we order this week it will arrive within 3 weeks.

docs.title: Appendix - Charts Example
docs.text:
graph LR
  A[Start] --> B{Error?};
  B -->|Yes| C[Hmm...];
  C --> D[Debug];
  D --> B;
  B ---->|No| E[Yay!];
sequenceDiagram
  autonumber
  Alice->>John: Hello John, how are you?
  loop Healthcheck
      John->>John: Fight against hypochondria
  end
  Note right of John: Rational thoughts!
  John-->>Alice: Great!
  John->>Bob: How about you?
  Bob-->>John: Jolly good!
stateDiagram-v2
  state fork_state <<fork>>
    [*] --> fork_state
    fork_state --> State2
    fork_state --> State3

    state join_state <<join>>
    State2 --> join_state
    State3 --> join_state
    join_state --> State4
    State4 --> [*]
